lessons.title: Wrapper Classes
lessons.summary: Understand wrapper classes corresponding to primitive data types and the mechanism of Autoboxing and Unboxing

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The role of **wrapper classes** (Integer, Double, etc.)
- The difference between valueOf() and parseXxx() [[methods]]
- How **autoboxing** and **unboxing** work

These are important [[classes]] that bridge primitive types and object types.

contents.title: Primitive Types and Their Wrapper Classes
contents.body:
Each of Java's 8 primitive [[data types]] has a corresponding **wrapper class**.

| Primitive Type | Wrapper Class |
|---|---|
| byte | Byte |
| short | Short |
| int | Integer |
| long | Long |
| float | Float |
| double | Double |
| char | Character |
| boolean | Boolean |

Note that the wrapper for int is Integer, and for char it is Character.
All other wrapper classes simply capitalize the first letter of the primitive type name.
All wrapper classes belong to the java.lang package and can be used without import statements.
contents.note: Pay attention to the spelling: Integer (not Int) and Character (not Char).

contents.title: How Autoboxing and Unboxing Work
contents.body:
Autoboxing is the automatic conversion from a primitive data type to its corresponding **wrapper class**.
Conversely, unboxing is the automatic conversion from a wrapper class to a primitive data type.
Since Java 5, the compiler performs these conversions automatically.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Note the Difference Between valueOf() and parseXxx()
contents.body:
Since these are often confused, let's organize them in a table.

| Method | Return Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Integer.valueOf("123") | Integer (wrapper type) | Returns an object |
| Integer.parseInt("123") | int (primitive type) | Returns a primitive |

Both convert a string to a number, but the return type is different.

Passing a string that cannot be converted to a number throws a NumberFormatException.
contents.note: On exams, you may also be asked about Integer.valueOf()'s cache for values -128 to 127.

contents.title: The parse and valueOf Methods
contents.body:
**Wrapper classes** provide parse methods that convert strings to numeric values, and valueOf methods that return wrapper objects.
Integer.parseInt converts a string to an int (primitive data type).
Integer.valueOf converts a string to an Integer (wrapper object).
Passing a string that cannot be converted to a number throws a NumberFormatException.
Similar methods are available in each wrapper class.
contents.note: Boolean.parseBoolean returns true only when the argument is "true" (case-insensitive); all other values return false. No [[exception]] is thrown.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Each primitive data type has a corresponding **wrapper class** (int → Integer, char → Character)
- Autoboxing is the automatic conversion from primitive to wrapper; unboxing is the reverse
- Integer.parseInt converts a string to int; Integer.valueOf converts a string to Integer
- Passing a non-convertible string throws a NumberFormatException
- Boolean.parseBoolean never throws an exception; everything other than "true" returns false

quizzes.question: Which is the wrapper class corresponding to int?
quizzes.explanation: The wrapper class for int is Integer. Int is not a class that exists in Java. Unlike byte → Byte, short → Short, and long → Long where you simply capitalize the first letter, int → Integer and char → Character have special names.

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: Integer a = 10 autoboxes the int value 10 into an Integer type. In a + 20, a is unboxed back to an int value, and 10 + 20 = 30 is calculated. As a result, 30 is assigned to b, and 30 is printed.

quizzes.question:
Complete the code to convert the string "100" to an int type.
int num = Integer.______("100");
quizzes.explanation: Integer.parseInt() is a static method that converts a string to a primitive int. It parses the given string as a base-10 number and returns an int value. If the string cannot be parsed as a number, a NumberFormatException is thrown.

lessons.title: Key Methods of the String Class
lessons.summary: Understand the frequently used methods of the String class and the concept of immutability

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The meaning of String **immutability**
- Key [[methods]] such as charAt, substring, indexOf, and replace
- Proper usage of string comparison (equals vs ==)

This is the most frequently tested API group on both exams and in practice.

contents.title: Basic String Methods
contents.body:
The String class has many useful methods.
length() returns the length of the string, and charAt() returns the character at a specified position.
substring() retrieves a substring, and indexOf() searches for the position of a character.
Note that indices start from 0 in these methods.
contents.note: Note that the endIndex in substring(beginIndex, endIndex) is exclusive. s.substring(0, 5) returns characters at indices 0 through 4.

contents.title: Understanding String Immutability
contents.body:
Once a String object is created, its contents cannot be changed.

- str.toUpperCase() → returns a new String (the original str is unchanged)
- str.replace("a", "b") → returns a new String (the original str is unchanged)
- str + "append" → returns a new String (the original str is unchanged)

If you don't reassign the return value to a [[variable]], the result of the operation is lost.
contents.note: A common exam question asks what the value of str is after String str = "hello"; str.toUpperCase(); (Answer: still "hello").

contents.title: The Difference Between equals() and ==
contents.body:
When comparing Strings, you need to accurately understand the difference between equals() and ==.
The == [[operator]] compares whether two objects have the same "reference."
The equals() method compares whether the string "contents" are the same.
Strings created with [[literals]] are stored in the string pool, so == may return true.
However, strings created with new String() have different references, so == returns false.
Always use equals() when comparing string contents.
The compareTo method compares strings lexicographically, returning 0 if equal, a negative value if less, and a positive value if greater.
contents.note: Exam questions frequently test the difference between == and equals(). Always use equals() for string comparison.

contents.title: String Immutability
contents.body:
Once a String object is created, its contents cannot be modified.
Even when methods like toUpperCase() and replace() are called, the original string remains unchanged, and a new String object is returned.
This property is called "immutable."
To use the modified string, you must assign the return value to a variable.
contents.note: 【Practical Tips】In business applications, String's immutability makes it safe to use as [[HashMap]] keys and for sharing between [[threads]]. For extensive string concatenation, use [[StringBuilder]] for better performance — this is a fundamental practice in the field.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| length() | Returns the string's length; charAt(i) returns the character at index i (0-based) |
| substring(begin, end) | Returns a substring from begin to before end |
| indexOf() | Returns the position of a character/string, or -1 if not found |
- == compares references; equals() compares contents. Use equals() for string comparison
- String is immutable — method calls do not change the original string

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: substring(4, 10) extracts characters from index 4 to index 9. In "JavaSilver", index 4 is 'S' and index 9 is 'r', so "Silver" is output. The key point is that endIndex 10 is exclusive.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes String immutability?
quizzes.explanation: String objects are immutable — calling methods like toUpperCase() or replace() does not change the original string. These methods create and return a new String object. To change the original, you must reassign the return value to a variable.

choices.text: Calling toUpperCase() changes the original string to uppercase
String methods return a new String object without modifying the original
String is a final class so it cannot be inherited, but its contents can be modified
Calling concat() appends a string to the end of the original string

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: s1 is created with a literal and stored in the string pool. s2 is created with the new operator, so a separate object is created on the heap. == compares references, so it returns false since they are different objects. equals() compares contents, so it returns true since both are "Hello".

quizzes.question: Complete the code for extracting a substring and converting it to uppercase.
quizzes.explanation: Use substring(start, end) to extract a substring and toUpperCase() to convert it to uppercase. Since String is immutable, a new instance is returned.

lessons.title: StringBuilder
lessons.summary: Understand how to use the StringBuilder class for mutable string operations and its differences from String

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The role of [[StringBuilder]] (mutable string operations)
- The append, insert, delete, and reverse methods
- Criteria for choosing between String and StringBuilder

StringBuilder is overwhelmingly faster for large-scale string concatenation.

contents.title: Basic StringBuilder Operations
contents.body:
Let's look at the key methods of StringBuilder.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| append() | Appends a string to the end. |
| insert() | Inserts a string at the specified position. |
| delete() | Deletes characters in the specified range. |
| reverse() | Reverses the entire string. |
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Method Chaining
contents.body:
Methods like append() and insert() in StringBuilder return a reference to the StringBuilder itself.
This allows you to chain multiple method calls together using "." notation.
This is called "method chaining" and offers the benefit of more concise code.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: When to Use String vs StringBuilder
contents.body:
You need to choose between String and StringBuilder depending on the situation.

【When String is appropriate】
- When you rarely modify strings
- When [[thread]] safety is needed (String is thread-safe because it is immutable)
- When only a small number of string concatenations are needed

【When StringBuilder is appropriate】
- When concatenating strings repeatedly inside a loop
- When frequently inserting or deleting strings
- When performance is critical

There is also a [[class]] called StringBuffer. It has the same functionality as StringBuilder but is thread-safe.
StringBuilder is more likely to appear on certification exams.
contents.note: StringBuilder is not thread-safe. Use StringBuffer in multithreaded environments.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- StringBuilder is mutable — the original object is modified directly
- append() adds to the end, insert() inserts at a position, delete() removes a range, reverse() reverses
- Method chaining allows multiple operations in a single statement
- StringBuilder is more efficient than String for string concatenation within loops
- Note that StringBuilder is not thread-safe (StringBuffer is thread-safe)

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: delete(1, 3) removes characters from index 1 up to (but not including) index 3 — that is, indexes 1 and 2. In "ABCDE", index 1 is 'B' and index 2 is 'C', so these are removed, leaving "ADE".

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between StringBuilder and String?
quizzes.explanation: String is immutable, so a new object is created each time a string operation is performed. StringBuilder, on the other hand, is mutable and modifies its internal buffer directly, making it efficient for repeated string modifications. StringBuilder is not thread-safe.

choices.text: StringBuilder is immutable and String is mutable
StringBuilder is mutable and the original object is modified directly
StringBuilder is thread-safe and recommended for multithreaded environments
StringBuilder methods always return void, so chaining is not possible

quizzes.question:
What is the method name that reverses a StringBuilder's string?
sb.______();
quizzes.explanation: reverse() is a method that reverses the contents of a StringBuilder. For example, applying reverse() to "Java" produces "avaJ". This method returns the StringBuilder itself, so it can be used in method chaining.

quizzes.question: Select all correct characteristics of the String class.
quizzes.explanation: String is immutable — once created, it cannot be changed. String literals are shared in the String Pool, and the same literal references the same object. Each concatenation with the + operator creates a new object, so StringBuilder is recommended for concatenation in loops. String is a final class and cannot be inherited.

choices.text: String literals are shared in the String Pool
You can create subclasses to extend its functionality
It is defined as a final class

quizzes.question: Arrange the steps for building a string with StringBuilder in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: StringBuilder follows the flow: create → append to add → toString() to convert to String.

lessons.title: Key ArrayList Operations
lessons.summary: Understand ArrayList's basic operations, generics, and processing elements with the enhanced for loop

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Key [[ArrayList]] operations (add, get, set, remove)
- Safe element removal using [[Iterator]]
- The danger of removing elements during iteration with a for-each loop

ArrayList is the most commonly used collection in practice.

contents.title: Basic ArrayList Operations
contents.body:
Let's look at creating an ArrayList and its basic [[methods]].
add() adds elements, and get() retrieves an element by index.
set() replaces an element at a specified position, and remove() deletes an element.
size() returns the current number of elements.
contents.note: When an int value is passed to remove(), it is treated as an index; when an object is passed, it removes by value.

contents.title: Do Not Call remove Inside a for-each Loop
contents.body:
The following code will throw a ConcurrentModificationException:

for ([[String]] s : list) { if (condition) list.remove(s); }

Safe removal methods:
- Use Iterator's remove() method
- Use list.removeIf(condition) (Java 8+, most recommended)
- Use a reverse index loop to remove elements

removeIf() allows you to specify conditions with [[lambda expressions]] and is the most concise approach.

contents.title: ArrayList Operations with the Enhanced For Loop (for-each)
contents.body:
Using the [[enhanced for loop]], you can concisely iterate over all elements of an ArrayList.
You can access each element without worrying about indices, making the code more readable.
However, modifying the list (adding or removing elements) during iteration throws a ConcurrentModificationException.
A regular [[for loop]] allows more flexible operations using indices.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Generics Basics
contents.body:
You can specify [[generics]] (type parameters) for ArrayList to restrict the type of elements it can store.
Declaring ArrayList<String> allows only String-type elements to be stored.
Primitive [[data types]] (such as int and double) cannot be used as type parameters.
Use **wrapper classes** (such as Integer and Double) instead.
Specifying generics ensures type safety at [[compile]] time and eliminates the need for [[casting]] when retrieving elements.
The [[diamond operator]] <> allows you to omit the type parameter on the right side.
contents.note: ArrayList<int> causes a compilation error. You must write ArrayList<Integer>.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- ArrayList is a variable-length list whose size changes as elements are added or removed
- add() adds, get() retrieves, set() replaces, remove() deletes, size() returns the element count
- Use generics to specify types; primitive data types cannot be used — **wrapper classes** are required
- The enhanced for loop can concisely iterate over all elements
- Modifying a list during a for-each loop throws a ConcurrentModificationException

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: First, list = [A, B, C]. remove(1) removes "B" at index 1, resulting in list = [A, C]. Then get(1) retrieves "C" at index 1. The key point is that elements shift after a removal.

quizzes.question: The following code contains an error. What is the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: Primitive types (int, double, etc.) cannot be used as generic type parameters for ArrayList. ArrayList<int> is a compilation error. You must write ArrayList<Integer> instead. With wrapper classes, autoboxing allows you to pass int values directly to add().

choices.text: Primitive types cannot be used in ArrayList<int>. It must be ArrayList<Integer>
The import statement for ArrayList is missing
int values cannot be passed directly to the add() method
The return value of get(0) must be received as an int type

quizzes.question: Which method retrieves the number of elements in an ArrayList?
quizzes.explanation: To get the number of elements in an ArrayList, use the size() method. Be careful not to confuse it with the length field for arrays (arr.length) or the length() method for Strings. The methods count() and getSize() do not exist in ArrayList.

lessons.title: LocalDate / LocalDateTime
lessons.summary: Understand date and time operations and formatting using LocalDate and LocalDateTime from the java.time package

contents.title: What You Will Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- LocalDate (date only), LocalTime (time only), and [[LocalDateTime]] (both)
- How to create, add, compare, and format dates and times
- Format conversion using [[DateTimeFormatter]]

You'll learn the basics of the new Date/Time API introduced in Java 8.

contents.title: Creating LocalDate and Key Methods
contents.body:
LocalDate is a [[class]] that represents a date (year, month, day).
Use now() to get the current date and of() to create a specific date.
getYear(), getMonthValue(), and getDayOfMonth() return individual date components.
plusDays() and minusMonths() allow date arithmetic.
Like String, LocalDate is immutable, so operations return new objects.
contents.note: The month in LocalDate.of() is specified as a number from 1 to 12 (not zero-based).

contents.title: LocalDate Is Designed to Be Immutable
contents.body:
Like String, [[methods]] of LocalDate do not modify the original object.

- date.plusDays(1) → returns a new LocalDate
- date.withMonth(12) → returns a new LocalDate

Be careful: if you don't capture the return value, the result is lost.

Also, you cannot use [[constructors]]; objects are created using of() and now().

contents.title: Working with LocalDateTime
contents.body:
LocalDateTime is a class that holds both a date and a time.
Use the of() method to create an [[instance]] by specifying year, month, day, hour, minute, and second.
plusHours() and minusMinutes() allow time arithmetic.
Like LocalDate, it is immutable.
contents.note: The "//" in the code denotes comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Formatting with DateTimeFormatter
contents.body:
DateTimeFormatter allows you to convert dates and times to custom-formatted strings.
Create a formatter by passing a pattern string to the ofPattern method.
The main pattern characters are as follows:

yyyy: Year (4 digits)
MM: Month (2 digits)
dd: Day (2 digits)
HH: Hour (24-hour format, 2 digits)
mm: Minute (2 digits)
ss: Second (2 digits)

Use the format() method to convert a LocalDate or LocalDateTime to a string.
Conversely, the parse() method can create a LocalDate from a string.
contents.note: M for month is uppercase; m for minute is lowercase. Be careful not to mix them up.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- LocalDate handles dates only; LocalDateTime handles both dates and times
- Objects are created using now() or of() (factory methods), not the new operator
- Arithmetic methods like plusDays() and minusHours() return new objects
- LocalDate/LocalDateTime are immutable — the original object is never changed
- DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern() enables custom format string conversion

quizzes.question: Which is the correct way to create a LocalDate instance?
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate cannot be created with the new operator. Use factory methods such as LocalDate.of() or LocalDate.now(). LocalDate.of(2025, 1, 15) returns a LocalDate object representing January 15, 2025. The constructor is private, so new LocalDate() causes a compilation error.

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: LocalDate is immutable. date.plusDays(10) returns a new LocalDate object, but since the return value is not assigned to a variable, the original date remains unchanged. Therefore, date.getDayOfMonth() returns the original value of 20.

quizzes.question:
Specify the pattern for "year-month-day" format (e.g., 2025-06-15) with DateTimeFormatter.
DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("______");
quizzes.explanation: In DateTimeFormatter patterns, yyyy represents a 4-digit year, MM represents a 2-digit month, and dd represents a 2-digit day. Using yyyy-MM-dd produces output like "2025-06-15". Note that M for month is uppercase — lowercase mm means "minutes."
